policy_statement: The City of Luxor will create a revolving fund to replace old street lighting lamps with LED, reinvesting savings to achieve full replacement over time.
mechanism_description: The city plans to use a revolving fund to replace old street lighting lamps with more efficient LEDs, reducing electricity consumption and reinvesting the resulting savings to fund additional replacements over time.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (street lighting) → ↓ electricity input → ↓ fuel combustion for power → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipal street-lighting infrastructure is upgraded from older, less efficient lamps/ballasts to LEDs (often with improved drivers/controls) while maintaining comparable illumination levels.  
2) Mediating physical process: LEDs provide more lumens per watt and better optical control, reducing electrical power set and total kWh consumed over operating hours.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced electricity demand lowers marginal electricity generation and associated CO₂e emissions (with magnitude depending on grid emissions factor), yielding mitigation benefits via efficiency.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced grid electricity consumption for public lighting, leading to avoided power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Efficiency retrofit delivers the same lighting service with less electricity, which reduces upstream generation and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although the objective is ultimately emissions reduction, the direct mechanism is reduced energy input per service (efficiency), which aligns best with Resource Efficiency rather than a direct fuel-switching Mitigation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is already near-zero-carbon, emissions benefits shrink but the mechanism remains energy-efficiency; if the retrofit includes major smart controls, it still primarily acts through electricity reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any “street lighting LED retrofit → electricity use reduction” policy primarily operates by lowering kWh for lighting, irrespective of financing method (e.g., revolving fund, ESCO).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., municipal operations, public lighting controls, financing type) requires policy-specific evidence and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the local grid is fully renewable/nuclear, the mitigation impact (avoided CO₂e) may be small, but the primary mechanism remains energy/resource efficiency; rebound effects are minimal because lighting levels are typically regulated, though “over-lighting” could partially offset savings in some implementations.
key_indicators: "revolving fund"; "initial financial support"; "dedicated to old lamps replacement"; "budget monitoring mechanism"; "put aside financial resources preserved due to more efficient lamps"; "Resources saved will be invested again in lamp replacement"
co_benefits: Improved lighting quality and safety; reduced municipal operating costs/budget relief; potential reduced maintenance due to longer lamp life
instance_edge_case_notes: Although it results in an equipment upgrade (infrastructure change), the policy lever described is the financing/reinvestment mechanism; classification therefore favors an incentive/financing program over infrastructure-investment as the instrument type.
